architecture_note: Approved content only. Runtime AI generation is not allowed. Search may retrieve approved cards only.
content_rules: Use universal language in displayed fields so scripts work across family structures and situations.
Do not use relationship-specific words in displayed fields such as sister, brother, mom, dad, baby, friend, classmate, or teacher.
Hidden search terms may include words parents might type, but displayed scripts must stay broadly applicable.
The app must never generate new parenting scripts, actions, helpful notes, or safety notes at runtime.
The Show another phrase button must rotate only through approved cards with the same category_id, behavior_id, age_band_id, and context_id.
This content is a quick draft for tough moments, not therapy, diagnosis, medical advice, or legal advice.

category.label: Sensory & Body Struggles
category.subtitle: Meet the body where it is

age_bands.tone_note: simple choice, low words, concrete next step
respectful, specific, collaborative without negotiating safety

contexts.label: Calm this down
contexts.description: Use when the child is overwhelmed, resisting, melting down, or escalating but there is not active danger.

contexts.label: Stop unsafe behavior
contexts.description: Use when the body care or sensory struggle has become unsafe, unsanitary, physically aggressive, or likely to cause harm.

contexts.label: Repair afterward
contexts.description: Use after everyone is safe enough to reconnect, reduce shame, clean up, or make a plan for next time.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: The clothes feel wrong. We can fix one thing. First, breathe with me.
cards.do_this: Pause the dressing demand. Check the tag, seam, sock, or waistband and offer one simple swap if possible.
cards.helpful_note: A sensory reaction is not usually stubbornness. Solving one body problem first lowers the fight.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: Your body says no to these clothes. We will slow down and try again.
cards.do_this: Lower your voice, loosen the clothing if safe, and give the child one softer option or a short break.
cards.helpful_note: For a young child, the discomfort can feel urgent. Calm body help works better than convincing.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: That feels too scratchy. I will help your body get comfortable.
cards.do_this: Name the sensation and help change one thing: turn socks, remove a tag, pick a soft layer, or take a brief pause.
cards.helpful_note: Naming the body problem helps the child feel understood without making the clothing optional forever.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: I will not let bodies get hurt. We are pausing the clothes right now.
cards.do_this: Move sharp objects and hard items away. Stop trying to dress the child while the body is thrashing or hitting.
cards.helpful_note: When the body is unsafe, the routine pauses. Safety comes before being on time.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: Your body is too upset for clothes right now. I am making space.
cards.do_this: Step back, keep the child covered as needed, and remove extra demands until the body slows down.
cards.helpful_note: Crowding a sensory meltdown can make the child fight harder. Space can reduce panic.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: Stop. Clothes can wait. People stay safe first.
cards.do_this: Block hitting or kicking if needed, then switch to the shortest safe plan: soft clothes, blanket, or a later retry.
cards.helpful_note: This protects safety without shaming the sensory response.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: That was hard on your body. Next time, we will check the clothes first.
cards.do_this: When calm, choose one preferred clothing option and put it in an easy-to-find place.
cards.helpful_note: Repair after sensory distress should be practical, not shame-based.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: Your body felt uncomfortable. We can make a better clothing plan.
cards.do_this: Make a simple plan: soft socks, no tags, backup outfit, or getting dressed in smaller steps.
cards.helpful_note: The child learns that body signals can be handled without a battle.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: You were not trying to be bad. The clothes felt wrong. We will try a safer way next time.
cards.do_this: Practice one phrase the child can use later, such as 'too scratchy' or 'help please.'
cards.helpful_note: Replacement words help prevent the next meltdown from becoming physical.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: The clothes feel wrong. We can fix one problem, then keep going.
cards.do_this: Ask for one body clue: tag, seam, waistband, socks, or temperature. Fix one thing and return to the next step.
cards.helpful_note: One specific question keeps the child from getting lost in the whole routine.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: I believe your body is uncomfortable. We still need clothes. Let us choose the least-bad option.
cards.do_this: Offer two acceptable clothing choices and remove the argument about whether clothing is happening.
cards.helpful_note: Validation plus a limit keeps the parent from turning sensory discomfort into a power struggle.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: Your body needs comfort. The day still needs clothes. We can do both.
cards.do_this: Set out one soft option and one regular option. Let the child pick without adding more talk.
cards.helpful_note: This keeps dignity and direction together.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: I will help with the clothes, but I will not let hurting happen.
cards.do_this: Move back if the child is hitting, kicking, ripping, or throwing. Restart only after bodies are safe.
cards.helpful_note: Stopping the unsafe behavior matters more than finishing the task quickly.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: We are pausing. Clothes do not get solved by hurting people or breaking things.
cards.do_this: Use a calm pause. Keep one safe clothing option nearby and wait for the child to be able to choose.
cards.helpful_note: A pause is not giving in. It is preventing escalation.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: The clothes are a problem. Hurting is also a problem. Safety comes first.
cards.do_this: Clear the area and repeat one phrase instead of lecturing about the outfit.
cards.helpful_note: Two problems at once need one first step: safety.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: Your body had a hard time with those clothes. Let us make a plan before the next time.
cards.do_this: Pick one backup outfit and one phrase the child can use before yelling or throwing.
cards.helpful_note: A plan made while calm lowers shame and gives the child a way to ask sooner.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: The feeling was real. The unsafe part still needs repair.
cards.do_this: Help the child repair anything thrown, torn, or said harshly, then choose a sensory-friendly option.
cards.helpful_note: Repair should address impact without denying the body distress.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: Next time, say 'this feels wrong' before your body gets too big.
cards.do_this: Practice the words once and place the preferred clothing where the child can reach it.
cards.helpful_note: Practice makes the replacement response easier to access under stress.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: I believe something feels wrong on your body. Name the problem, and we will solve one piece.
cards.do_this: Give privacy if needed. Ask the child to identify the problem, then offer a practical fix or backup outfit.
cards.helpful_note: Older kids need respect and a path forward, not a debate about whether the feeling is real.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: We are not arguing about whether it bothers you. We are choosing the next workable option.
cards.do_this: Offer a short menu: change socks, remove tag, switch layer, or take a two-minute reset.
cards.helpful_note: This avoids invalidating sensory distress while keeping the routine moving.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: Your body is overwhelmed by the clothing. Let us make the smallest change that helps.
cards.do_this: Invite one adjustment, then return to the next step without revisiting the whole outfit.
cards.helpful_note: Small fixes keep the situation from becoming all-or-nothing.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: I will help solve the clothing problem. I will not stay close while bodies are unsafe.
cards.do_this: Create distance if the child is throwing, kicking, or tearing things. Keep your voice low and pause the conversation.
cards.helpful_note: Distance can prevent the sensory struggle from becoming a power struggle.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: This is too escalated. We are stopping the argument and keeping people safe.
cards.do_this: Remove breakable items and give one next step: space, soft clothes, or a quiet reset.
cards.helpful_note: When the brain is overloaded, reasoning usually stops working.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: The clothing can change. The unsafe behavior has to stop first.
cards.do_this: Do not keep negotiating during aggression. Repeat the safety line and wait for a calmer body.
cards.helpful_note: The child can be uncomfortable and still responsible for not hurting people.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: The sensory problem was real, and the escalation affected the room. Let us make a better plan.
cards.do_this: Talk through the trigger, the unsafe part, and one clothing plan for next time.
cards.helpful_note: This keeps accountability connected to problem-solving instead of shame.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: Next time, ask for the adjustment before it turns into a fight.
cards.do_this: Create a short clothing checklist: texture, tag, socks, temperature, backup option.
cards.helpful_note: A predictable checklist gives the child more control in a safe way.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: We need to repair what happened and protect your body comfort next time.
cards.do_this: Have the child repair any mess or hurt words, then choose a sensory-friendly plan together.
cards.helpful_note: Repair and accommodation can happen at the same time.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: I believe the clothing is bothering your body. Let us solve the sensory part without turning this into a fight.
cards.do_this: Give privacy and time to adjust. Offer a deadline and one acceptable backup option.
cards.helpful_note: Teens need respect for body discomfort and a clear expectation about moving forward.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: You do not have to prove it hurts. We do need a workable plan.
cards.do_this: Ask for the specific issue and collaborate on one solution that still meets the situation.
cards.helpful_note: This lowers defensiveness while keeping the parent from giving unlimited control.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: Your body comfort matters. The next step still matters. Let us find the middle.
cards.do_this: Offer a brief reset, then have the teen choose between two acceptable clothing plans.
cards.helpful_note: Choice works best when the parent defines the safe limits first.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: I will not argue while things are being thrown or damaged. We can solve clothing after the room is safe.
cards.do_this: Step back, keep breakable items away, and stop the clothing conversation until the unsafe behavior stops.
cards.helpful_note: This prevents the sensory issue from becoming a physical power struggle.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: I hear that the clothes feel unbearable. I still have to keep people and property safe.
cards.do_this: Use a calm limit and reduce the audience. Offer space, then return to the plan later.
cards.helpful_note: Respectful limits are especially important with teens.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: We are pausing this conversation. Safety first, clothing plan second.
cards.do_this: Do not follow, corner, or lecture. Give space and return when the teen can talk safely.
cards.helpful_note: A pause protects dignity and reduces escalation.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: The sensory part was real. The way it came out still needs repair.
cards.do_this: Talk through what helped, what crossed a line, and what the teen can ask for next time.
cards.helpful_note: This keeps support and accountability together.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: Let us build a plan that respects your body and does not blow up the whole routine.
cards.do_this: Create a private clothing plan with acceptable alternatives and timing expectations.
cards.helpful_note: Planning ahead gives the teen more control without removing all structure.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: Next time, I want to hear the problem earlier, before it turns unsafe.
cards.do_this: Agree on a phrase, signal, or backup outfit that can be used without a long argument.
cards.helpful_note: A simple signal can prevent public conflict and preserve dignity.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: Mouth care feels hard. We will do a tiny brush, then stop.
cards.do_this: Use the smallest step possible: touch toothbrush to lips, brush one tooth, or wipe teeth with a cloth if appropriate.
cards.helpful_note: Starting smaller keeps the routine from becoming a battle.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: Your mouth says no. I will go slow. One little swipe.
cards.do_this: Let the child hold the brush or choose water first. Keep the brushing brief and predictable.
cards.helpful_note: Mouth sensations can feel intense. Predictability helps the body trust the routine.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: Teeth need care. We can make it quick and gentle.
cards.do_this: Use a calm voice, count a few seconds, and stop before the child is fully overwhelmed when possible.
cards.helpful_note: Ending while the child is still somewhat regulated builds tolerance over time.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: I will not let the toothbrush hurt anyone. We are pausing.
cards.do_this: Move the toothbrush away if it is being grabbed, thrown, or used to jab. Try again only after bodies are safe.
cards.helpful_note: Pausing protects safety and keeps toothbrushing from feeling like a fight.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: Stop. The brush goes down. Bodies stay safe.
cards.do_this: Set the brush out of reach and help the child settle with water, breathing, or a short break.
cards.helpful_note: The hygiene task can wait a moment. Safety cannot.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: Your body is too upset for brushing right now. We will reset first.
cards.do_this: Step back and reduce pressure. Return with a smaller step if the child can tolerate it.
cards.helpful_note: A reset prevents the routine from teaching panic.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: That was hard. Next time we will start smaller.
cards.do_this: Practice touching the toothbrush to the hand or lips for one second while calm.
cards.helpful_note: Practice outside the hard moment helps the mouth-care routine feel less threatening.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: Your mouth did not like that. We will try a gentler plan.
cards.do_this: Choose one change: different toothpaste, water first, shorter brushing, or a song/countdown.
cards.helpful_note: Small adjustments can reduce sensory overload without dropping the routine.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: Teeth still need care. We will help your body learn it slowly.
cards.do_this: End with connection and choose the next smallest step for tomorrow.
cards.helpful_note: The goal is not perfect brushing today. The goal is progress without shame.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: Brushing feels hard right now. Choose: water first or brush first.
cards.do_this: Offer one simple choice and keep the brushing short. Avoid explaining germs in the middle of refusal.
cards.helpful_note: One choice gives control without turning hygiene into a negotiation.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: Your mouth feels bothered. We still need tooth care. Let us do the first ten seconds.
cards.do_this: Use a timer or count calmly. Stop for a breath, then continue if possible.
cards.helpful_note: A short, visible finish line makes the task feel survivable.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: You do not have to like it. We do have to care for your teeth.
cards.do_this: Keep the boundary steady and offer a tolerable option: different paste, no paste, or child brushes first.
cards.helpful_note: This keeps the limit without shaming the sensory discomfort.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: The toothbrush is not for throwing or hurting. We are pausing.
cards.do_this: Take space and place the toothbrush down. Restart with one small step only when the child is safe.
cards.helpful_note: Unsafe behavior should stop before the hygiene task continues.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: I will help with teeth. I will not let bodies get hurt.
cards.do_this: Move back if the child swings, kicks, or spits aggressively. Keep words short.
cards.helpful_note: The parent can keep the routine important without forcing it during unsafe escalation.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: Stop. We are making bodies safe before we try again.
cards.do_this: Use water, a washcloth, or a shorter plan if the child can reset. Otherwise pause and return later.
cards.helpful_note: A flexible route protects the relationship and still points back to care.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: Brushing got too big. Let us make a better plan for next time.
cards.do_this: Ask which part felt hardest: taste, brush, water, timing, or control. Pick one adjustment.
cards.helpful_note: Finding the hard part helps the next routine start with less resistance.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: The feeling was real. Throwing and yelling did not help your body get care.
cards.do_this: Repair any mess, then practice the first step calmly for a few seconds.
cards.helpful_note: Brief practice creates a new path without forcing a long apology.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: Next time, say 'my mouth needs a break' before your body gets too upset.
cards.do_this: Practice the phrase and agree on one short break during brushing.
cards.helpful_note: A planned break can reduce panic and increase cooperation.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: I hear that brushing feels awful. We are going to make it doable, not optional.
cards.do_this: Ask the child to choose the least-hard version: different toothpaste, timer, rinse first, or brush in sections.
cards.helpful_note: Respecting the sensory issue lowers the need to fight while keeping hygiene expected.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: Let us solve the hardest part instead of arguing about the whole routine.
cards.do_this: Have the child name the trigger, then set a brief timer for the first step.
cards.helpful_note: Specific problem-solving works better than a broad lecture about responsibility.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: Your mouth is sensitive. Teeth still need care. Pick the first step.
cards.do_this: Offer two acceptable starting points and stay calm if the child complains.
cards.helpful_note: This gives ownership without giving the routine away.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: I will not continue while the toothbrush is being thrown or used unsafely.
cards.do_this: Set the toothbrush aside and create distance. Return after the child can handle the tool safely.
cards.helpful_note: The tool only returns when it can be used safely.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: This is too escalated for brushing. We are pausing and keeping bodies safe.
cards.do_this: Stop the back-and-forth. Use a short reset, then restart with the smallest step.
cards.helpful_note: Pausing is a safety choice, not a reward.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: Mouth care matters. Safety comes first.
cards.do_this: Remove the audience and reduce stimulation. Do not keep arguing from the doorway.
cards.helpful_note: A lower-stimulation restart helps the child regain control.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: We need a tooth-care plan that does not turn into a fight every time.
cards.do_this: Make a two-step plan the child can follow: first rinse, then brush one section, then continue.
cards.helpful_note: A concrete plan helps reduce avoidance and parent-child friction.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: The sensory part is real. The unsafe part still needs repair.
cards.do_this: Repair any mess or hurtful words, then choose one adjustment for the next brushing.
cards.helpful_note: This separates discomfort from behavior.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: Next time, ask for a modification before refusing the whole thing.
cards.do_this: List acceptable modifications and write or save the plan where it can be seen.
cards.helpful_note: Pre-approved options reduce repeated negotiation.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: I understand this feels unpleasant. Dental care still has to happen. What is the most workable version tonight?
cards.do_this: Offer autonomy within limits: product choice, timing, timer, or sequence. Do not micromanage if safety is not an issue.
cards.helpful_note: Teens respond better when the boundary is clear and their dignity is preserved.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: This is about care, not control. Choose the version you can follow through on.
cards.do_this: Let the teen choose a reasonable routine and confirm when it will be done.
cards.helpful_note: Ownership reduces power struggles around body care.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: I am not going to argue about whether it matters. Let us make the routine tolerable.
cards.do_this: Ask for one barrier and one solution. Keep the tone practical, not parental-scolding.
cards.helpful_note: Practical problem-solving keeps the teen engaged.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: I will not keep discussing this while things are being thrown or people are being threatened.
cards.do_this: Step away, keep the bathroom safe, and return to the hygiene expectation later.
cards.helpful_note: Escalation should not become the way to escape every body-care routine.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: We are pausing this conversation until it can stay safe.
cards.do_this: Do not follow or corner. Give space, then restate the expectation later in one sentence.
cards.helpful_note: Space protects safety and respect.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: The routine can be adjusted. Unsafe behavior cannot continue.
cards.do_this: End the argument. Return with a written or simple verbal plan when calm.
cards.helpful_note: A delayed reset is more effective than forcing compliance during escalation.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: We need a plan for dental care that respects your sensory needs and does not become a fight.
cards.do_this: Agree on products, timing, and minimum routine. Keep it specific enough to follow.
cards.helpful_note: A clear plan reduces daily conflict and preserves dignity.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: The refusal made sense as a signal. The escalation still needs a reset.
cards.do_this: Name what crossed the line and what support would help next time.
cards.helpful_note: This turns the incident into a plan instead of a shame spiral.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: Next time, ask for a change before the routine turns into conflict.
cards.do_this: Create a short list of acceptable changes and decide when the routine will happen.
cards.helpful_note: Predictable options reduce control battles.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: Hair brushing feels ouchy. We will go slow and do one small part.
cards.do_this: Start at the ends, use gentle hands, and stop after a tiny section if needed.
cards.helpful_note: A young child may experience tangles as real pain. Going smaller builds trust.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: Your head feels upset. I will be gentle. One brush, then a break.
cards.do_this: Show the brush, count one or two strokes, then pause before the child panics.
cards.helpful_note: Predictable pauses help the child tolerate body care.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: We can help your hair without rushing your body.
cards.do_this: Use detangler or fingers first if available, and keep words soft and brief.
cards.helpful_note: Lower pressure makes the task feel less threatening.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: I will not let the brush hurt anyone. We are pausing.
cards.do_this: Put the brush down if the child swings, throws, or grabs. Create space and restart later with one small step.
cards.helpful_note: Body care should not become a physical fight.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: Stop. The brush goes away until bodies are safe.
cards.do_this: Move the tool out of reach and help the child settle before trying again.
cards.helpful_note: Removing the tool protects safety without shaming the child.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: Hair can wait. Safe bodies come first.
cards.do_this: Pause the routine and offer a calming option before returning to hair care.
cards.helpful_note: Safety is the first step when sensory distress becomes physical.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: Hair brushing was hard. Next time we will start with a smaller part.
cards.do_this: When calm, practice one gentle stroke or choose a softer tool.
cards.helpful_note: Practice helps the child learn that hair care can happen without panic.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: Your head felt uncomfortable. We will make a gentler hair plan.
cards.do_this: Choose one support: detangler, holding the brush, starting at ends, or taking breaks.
cards.helpful_note: Giving the body a plan lowers fear next time.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: You were not bad. Your body was upset. We will try a safer way next time.
cards.do_this: Teach a simple phrase like 'gentle' or 'break please.'
cards.helpful_note: Replacement words help the child ask before melting down.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: Hair brushing feels hard. Choose: hold the brush or choose the first section.
cards.do_this: Give one choice and start gently at the ends. Avoid debating whether it hurts.
cards.helpful_note: Choice gives control while keeping hair care moving.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: I believe it hurts. We still need to care for your hair. We will do it in small parts.
cards.do_this: Use a timer, sections, or breaks. Keep the sequence predictable.
cards.helpful_note: Validation reduces the need to prove the discomfort.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: Your head needs gentle care. We will brush a little, then pause.
cards.do_this: Name each step before doing it and pause after a small section.
cards.helpful_note: Predictability reduces sensory panic.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: I will help your hair. I will not let hitting or throwing happen.
cards.do_this: Move the brush away and step back until bodies are safe.
cards.helpful_note: A pause prevents the task from becoming a battle over control.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: The brush is not for hurting. We are stopping and resetting.
cards.do_this: Set the brush down, reduce demands, and restart with a smaller step if possible.
cards.helpful_note: Tools need clear safety rules.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: Hair care can be gentle. Unsafe behavior has to stop first.
cards.do_this: Keep the limit brief. Do not force brushing while the child is thrashing.
cards.helpful_note: Forcing through the peak can make future hair care harder.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: That got too big. Let us make a gentler plan for next time.
cards.do_this: Ask what helped and what hurt, then pick one change for the next hair routine.
cards.helpful_note: The goal is cooperation without shame.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: The feeling was real. The unsafe part still needs repair.
cards.do_this: Repair any mess or hurt words, then practice asking for a break.
cards.helpful_note: Repair teaches responsibility without denying discomfort.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: Next time, say 'break please' before your body gets too upset.
cards.do_this: Practice the phrase and decide where breaks happen.
cards.helpful_note: A practiced phrase can prevent escalation.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: I believe your scalp is sensitive. Let us solve the hard part and keep moving.
cards.do_this: Ask the child to choose the order: detangle ends, use spray, section hair, or take a timed break.
cards.helpful_note: Respectful collaboration lowers defensiveness.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: We are not arguing about whether it hurts. We are finding the least painful way.
cards.do_this: Offer a tool choice or let the child brush the first section independently.
cards.helpful_note: This keeps hair care expected without dismissing pain.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: Your body needs more control over this. Choose the first step.
cards.do_this: Give two acceptable options and step back when possible.
cards.helpful_note: Older kids often cooperate better when they are not overhandled.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: I will not continue hair care while the brush is being used unsafely.
cards.do_this: Remove the tool and create distance. Return later with a smaller plan.
cards.helpful_note: Stopping the unsafe behavior protects everyone and keeps the tool neutral.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: This is too escalated. We are pausing the routine.
cards.do_this: Do not chase or force. Allow space, then restate the minimum hair-care step later.
cards.helpful_note: A calm pause prevents a sensory issue from becoming a power struggle.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: The hair problem can be solved. Hurting people or breaking things cannot continue.
cards.do_this: Repeat the safety line once and stop debating.
cards.helpful_note: The parent can hold the limit without arguing.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: Let us build a hair plan that does not end in a fight.
cards.do_this: Choose a routine: product, tool, timing, and who does which part.
cards.helpful_note: A clear plan reduces daily friction.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: The sensory part was real. The escalation affected the room.
cards.do_this: Repair anything damaged or said harshly, then adjust the routine.
cards.helpful_note: This keeps both care and accountability in place.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: Next time, ask for a modification before refusing the whole thing.
cards.do_this: Agree on acceptable modifications and write them down if helpful.
cards.helpful_note: Pre-approved choices reduce repeated arguments.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: I understand hair care feels uncomfortable. What is the most workable version right now?
cards.do_this: Offer privacy and autonomy. Let the teen choose tools, timing, or a minimum care step.
cards.helpful_note: Teens need body respect and clear expectations, not micromanaging.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: This is care, not control. Choose the plan you can actually follow through on.
cards.do_this: Ask for one barrier and one solution, then step back.
cards.helpful_note: Ownership lowers resistance.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: Your comfort matters. The routine still needs a plan.
cards.do_this: Collaborate on a realistic minimum: detangle, tie back, wash later, or use a protective option.
cards.helpful_note: A workable plan protects dignity and hygiene.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: I will not continue this conversation while things are unsafe.
cards.do_this: Step back and pause the routine. Return later with a simple expectation.
cards.helpful_note: Distance keeps the conflict from becoming physical.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: The routine can change. Unsafe behavior cannot continue.
cards.do_this: Do not follow or corner. Give space and reset when calm.
cards.helpful_note: Respectful space works better than forcing control.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: We are pausing. Safety first, hair plan later.
cards.do_this: End the argument and revisit the minimum care expectation at a calmer time.
cards.helpful_note: A pause can preserve the relationship and the boundary.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: We need a hair-care plan that respects your body and does not become a fight.
cards.do_this: Agree on products, timing, privacy, and minimum care expectations.
cards.helpful_note: A plan made calmly is easier to follow than one invented during conflict.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: The refusal told me something was not working. The escalation still needs repair.
cards.do_this: Name the crossed line and decide what support is needed next time.
cards.helpful_note: This keeps the teen from feeling shamed while still addressing impact.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: Next time, ask for an adjustment before the routine turns into conflict.
cards.do_this: Agree on the exact adjustment options and when care will happen.
cards.helpful_note: Clarity reduces ongoing power struggles.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: That food feels wrong in your mouth. You can spit it here. We will slow down.
cards.do_this: Offer a napkin or safe place to spit. Remove pressure and offer water.
cards.helpful_note: Gagging is a body signal. Staying neutral helps food stay less scary.
cards.safety_note: If the child is choking, struggling to breathe, or cannot stop vomiting, seek urgent help right away.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: Your mouth says no. We will not force that bite.
cards.do_this: Move the food back and offer a familiar safe food if available. Keep your face calm.
cards.helpful_note: Force can make texture fear stronger. Calm exposure works better than pressure.
cards.safety_note: If the child is choking, struggling to breathe, or cannot stop vomiting, seek urgent help right away.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: That texture surprised your body. Take a sip. We can try later.
cards.do_this: Give a small reset and avoid making the gagging the center of attention.
cards.helpful_note: Low attention prevents shame and helps the body settle.
cards.safety_note: If the child is choking, struggling to breathe, or cannot stop vomiting, seek urgent help right away.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: Stop. We are taking that bite out and helping your body breathe.
cards.do_this: Remove food from reach, offer a napkin, and watch breathing. Keep the child upright.
cards.helpful_note: Safety comes before finishing the meal.
cards.safety_note: If the child is choking, struggling to breathe, or cannot stop vomiting, seek urgent help right away.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: Your body is not ready for that food. I am moving it away.
cards.do_this: Pause the meal demand. Clean up calmly and offer water if appropriate.
cards.helpful_note: Pushing through gagging can escalate into vomiting or panic.
cards.safety_note: If the child is choking, struggling to breathe, or cannot stop vomiting, seek urgent help right away.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: No more bites right now. Your body needs a break.
cards.do_this: End the pressured bite and return to a safe food or regulation step.
cards.helpful_note: A break helps the child trust that body signals will be respected.
cards.safety_note: If the child is choking, struggling to breathe, or cannot stop vomiting, seek urgent help right away.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: That food was hard for your mouth. We can try a tiny step another time.
cards.do_this: When calm, decide on one low-pressure exposure: smell, touch, lick, or tiny bite.
cards.helpful_note: Tiny steps build tolerance without turning meals into fear.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: You were not bad. Your body had a strong reaction.
cards.do_this: Clean up without shame and move on to the next neutral step of the meal.
cards.helpful_note: Shame can make food struggles worse. Neutrality protects progress.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: Next time, say 'texture problem' before your body panics.
cards.do_this: Practice a simple phrase and agree on where food can be politely spit if needed.
cards.helpful_note: A planned exit lowers panic and messy reactions.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: That texture is hard for your mouth. You do not have to force it. Choose: smell, touch, or tiny taste.
cards.do_this: Offer a low-pressure step and keep the rest of the meal calm.
cards.helpful_note: Small exposure keeps food learning possible without triggering panic.
cards.safety_note: If the child is choking, struggling to breathe, or cannot stop vomiting, seek urgent help right away.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: Your body is gagging. We will pause and make this smaller.
cards.do_this: Move the food back, offer water, and avoid comments about being dramatic or rude.
cards.helpful_note: The child needs body safety before they can be curious about food.
cards.safety_note: If the child is choking, struggling to breathe, or cannot stop vomiting, seek urgent help right away.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: You can listen to your body. We still use kind food manners.
cards.do_this: Teach a neutral response: spit into a napkin, say 'no thank you,' or take a break.
cards.helpful_note: This protects both the child's dignity and the mealtime environment.
cards.safety_note: If the child is choking, struggling to breathe, or cannot stop vomiting, seek urgent help right away.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: Stop. No more forced bites. We are helping your body settle.
cards.do_this: Pause the food demand and clean up calmly if gagging or vomiting starts.
cards.helpful_note: Pressure during gagging often makes the body fight harder.
cards.safety_note: If the child is choking, struggling to breathe, or cannot stop vomiting, seek urgent help right away.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: Food stays safe. We are not throwing, shoving, or forcing bites.
cards.do_this: Move plates and utensils if they are being thrown or used unsafely.
cards.helpful_note: The meal can restart when the body and space are safe.
cards.safety_note: If the child is choking, struggling to breathe, or cannot stop vomiting, seek urgent help right away.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: Your mouth needs a break. We will come back when your body is calm.
cards.do_this: Give water, a napkin, and space. Do not lecture about wasting food right now.
cards.helpful_note: Safety and regulation come before teaching manners.
cards.safety_note: If the child is choking, struggling to breathe, or cannot stop vomiting, seek urgent help right away.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: That texture was too much. Let us make a plan for the next meal.
cards.do_this: Choose one acceptable response for hard textures and one tiny exposure step.
cards.helpful_note: Planning helps the child respond without panic or mess next time.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: The gagging was your body. The throwing or yelling still needs repair.
cards.do_this: Have the child help with a small cleanup, then reset without shame.
cards.helpful_note: Repair addresses impact while keeping food neutral.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: Next time, say 'too hard for my mouth' and use the napkin.
cards.do_this: Practice the phrase once and show where the napkin or safe spit spot is.
cards.helpful_note: Practiced words reduce panic behaviors.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: I hear that texture is not working for your body. We are going to keep this low-pressure.
cards.do_this: Offer a choice of sensory steps: look, smell, touch, lick, tiny bite, or skip for now if needed.
cards.helpful_note: Respect lowers defensiveness and keeps food from becoming a control battle.
cards.safety_note: If the child is choking, struggling to breathe, or cannot stop vomiting, seek urgent help right away.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: You do not have to pretend it is fine. Use a respectful exit and we will keep going.
cards.do_this: Remind the child to use a napkin, water, or a quiet break instead of dramatic reactions.
cards.helpful_note: This teaches body awareness and social safety without shame.
cards.safety_note: If the child is choking, struggling to breathe, or cannot stop vomiting, seek urgent help right away.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: Your mouth is giving a strong signal. Let us pause before this gets bigger.
cards.do_this: Pause the bite and redirect to a safe food or calming step.
cards.helpful_note: Stopping early can prevent vomiting, panic, or mealtime conflict.
cards.safety_note: If the child is choking, struggling to breathe, or cannot stop vomiting, seek urgent help right away.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: We are stopping the food pressure. Bodies and food need to stay safe.
cards.do_this: Move utensils or plates if they are being thrown. Keep your voice neutral and end the debate.
cards.helpful_note: An unsafe mealtime needs a safety reset, not a lecture.
cards.safety_note: If the child is choking, struggling to breathe, or cannot stop vomiting, seek urgent help right away.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: No more bites right now. Your body needs a reset.
cards.do_this: Watch for choking or vomiting, offer water if appropriate, and reduce attention.
cards.helpful_note: Keeping it calm prevents embarrassment from turning into escalation.
cards.safety_note: If the child is choking, struggling to breathe, or cannot stop vomiting, seek urgent help right away.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: Food is not for forcing, throwing, or proving a point. We are pausing.
cards.do_this: Clear the immediate space and return to the meal only when safe.
cards.helpful_note: Safety limits protect everyone while avoiding food shame.
cards.safety_note: If the child is choking, struggling to breathe, or cannot stop vomiting, seek urgent help right away.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: Let us separate the texture problem from the reaction. What is the plan next time?
cards.do_this: Identify the texture trigger and choose a respectful response before the next meal.
cards.helpful_note: This builds ownership without making the child feel broken.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: Your body reaction was real. The mealtime behavior still needs repair.
cards.do_this: Have the child help clean up and choose one tiny future exposure or approved alternative.
cards.helpful_note: Repair works best when it is practical and shame-free.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: Next time, signal early before your body gets overwhelmed.
cards.do_this: Choose a word, gesture, or napkin plan for hard textures.
cards.helpful_note: Early signals help prevent panic and public embarrassment.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: I believe the texture is hard for you. Let us handle it respectfully and keep the meal calm.
cards.do_this: Offer a discreet exit: napkin, water, skip that item, or choose a safe alternative.
cards.helpful_note: Teens need dignity around food and body responses.
cards.safety_note: If the child is choking, struggling to breathe, or cannot stop vomiting, seek urgent help right away.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: You do not have to force that food. You do need to handle the reaction safely.
cards.do_this: Set a calm expectation around language, cleanup, and safe alternatives.
cards.helpful_note: This avoids both food pressure and avoidance of responsibility.
cards.safety_note: If the child is choking, struggling to breathe, or cannot stop vomiting, seek urgent help right away.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: Your body is reacting. Take a pause before this becomes a bigger situation.
cards.do_this: Give privacy if needed and avoid public commentary.
cards.helpful_note: Privacy reduces shame and helps the teen regain control.
cards.safety_note: If the child is choking, struggling to breathe, or cannot stop vomiting, seek urgent help right away.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: We are pausing the meal. No one needs to force food or make the space unsafe.
cards.do_this: Remove pressure and clear utensils or plates if needed. Watch for choking, vomiting, or panic.
cards.helpful_note: The immediate goal is safety and dignity.
cards.safety_note: If the child is choking, struggling to breathe, or cannot stop vomiting, seek urgent help right away.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: Food cannot become a fight or a weapon. We are stopping this part now.
cards.do_this: End the debate and move to a safer setting if needed.
cards.helpful_note: Clear limits keep mealtime from escalating while respecting sensory needs.
cards.safety_note: If the child is choking, struggling to breathe, or cannot stop vomiting, seek urgent help right away.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: No more pressure right now. Your body needs a reset.
cards.do_this: Offer water, space, and a calm return plan if the teen is able.
cards.helpful_note: A regulated body can problem-solve better than a panicked one.
cards.safety_note: If the child is choking, struggling to breathe, or cannot stop vomiting, seek urgent help right away.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: Let us make a food plan that respects your body and keeps meals respectful.
cards.do_this: Agree on safe alternatives, how to decline a food, and what cleanup is expected.
cards.helpful_note: A clear plan protects dignity and reduces repeated conflict.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: The texture issue was real. The reaction still affected the space.
cards.do_this: Name what needs repair and decide what support helps next time.
cards.helpful_note: This keeps accountability from becoming shame.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: Next time, signal early and use the exit plan before it becomes a scene.
cards.do_this: Create a discreet signal or phrase and a plan for safe alternatives.
cards.helpful_note: Discreet signals help teens maintain dignity.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: That sound is too big. I will help your ears.
cards.do_this: Lower the noise if possible, cover ears gently if welcomed, or move to a quieter spot.
cards.helpful_note: Noise distress is a body alarm. Reducing the sound works better than explaining it away.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: Your ears need quiet. We are moving away.
cards.do_this: Pick the child up or guide them to a calmer area if safe.
cards.helpful_note: A young child often needs the adult to change the environment first.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: Too loud. Safe body. Quiet place.
cards.do_this: Use very few words and make your body calm and slow.
cards.helpful_note: Short language helps when the child cannot process more input.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: Stop. Running or hitting is not safe. I am helping your body move away.
cards.do_this: Block unsafe movement and guide the child to a quieter, safer location.
cards.helpful_note: The sound may be the trigger, but safety still needs a clear limit.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: The noise is too much. People stay safe while we get quiet.
cards.do_this: Move others back if needed and reduce demands until the child is away from the noise.
cards.helpful_note: Removing the trigger often reduces the unsafe behavior.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: I will keep your body safe. We are going to quiet now.
cards.do_this: Use calm physical guidance only as needed for immediate safety, then create space from the sound.
cards.helpful_note: Safety language should stay simple during sensory overload.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: That noise scared your body. Next time we will find quiet faster.
cards.do_this: When calm, practice covering ears, asking for quiet, or moving to a safe spot.
cards.helpful_note: Practice gives the child a plan before the body panics.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: Your ears had a hard time. We can help them next time.
cards.do_this: Prepare headphones, a quiet spot, or a signal for future noisy places.
cards.helpful_note: A plan reduces fear of the next noise.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: You were not bad. The sound was too much. We will use our quiet plan.
cards.do_this: Name the plan in one short sentence and move on without shame.
cards.helpful_note: Shame can make sensory alarms more intense.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: That sound is too much for your ears. Choose: headphones or quiet spot.
cards.do_this: Offer one sensory tool and one movement option.
cards.helpful_note: Simple choices restore control without forcing the child to stay overwhelmed.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: Your ears are overwhelmed. We can lower the sound or move away.
cards.do_this: Act quickly. Do not ask the child to tolerate noise just to prove they can.
cards.helpful_note: Meeting the body first helps the brain settle.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: I see the noise is hurting your body. Let us get safer and quieter.
cards.do_this: Guide the child away from the noise and reduce talking until they settle.
cards.helpful_note: Less talking means less sensory load.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: I will help with the noise. I will not let running or hurting happen.
cards.do_this: Move between the child and danger, then guide to a quieter place.
cards.helpful_note: The trigger is real, and the safety limit still stands.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: Stop. We are moving to quiet with safe feet and safe hands.
cards.do_this: Use a short phrase and a clear direction. Avoid arguing in the noisy place.
cards.helpful_note: The child may not be able to process reasoning until the noise is reduced.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: People stay safe while we get away from the sound.
cards.do_this: Clear a path and move away from crowds, doors, streets, or breakable items.
cards.helpful_note: Noise distress can cause flight behavior, so the first job is reducing risk.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: That sound got too big. Let us make a noise plan for next time.
cards.do_this: Choose a tool: headphones, signal, quiet break, or leaving sooner.
cards.helpful_note: A plan makes the child feel less trapped next time.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: The noise was hard. The unsafe part still needs repair.
cards.do_this: Repair any hurt or mess, then practice the signal for quiet.
cards.helpful_note: Repair teaches impact without blaming the sensory need.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: Next time, say 'too loud' before your body runs or hits.
cards.do_this: Practice the phrase or hand signal once.
cards.helpful_note: A simple signal can prevent escalation in public.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: I can see the sound is overloading your body. Use the noise plan now.
cards.do_this: Offer headphones, a quieter space, or a timed break without drawing attention.
cards.helpful_note: Older kids need dignity as much as support.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: You do not have to push through this noise. Choose the safest way to reset.
cards.do_this: Give two options: step out or use headphones. Keep it brief.
cards.helpful_note: Clear options help the child act before panic takes over.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: Your ears are overwhelmed. We are going to reduce input first.
cards.do_this: Lower sound, move away, or pause the activity. Do not start a lecture in the noise.
cards.helpful_note: Problem-solving comes after the sensory load drops.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: I will help you get away from the noise. I will not let you run into danger or hurt people.
cards.do_this: Stay near exits and guide the child away from traffic, crowds, or unsafe areas.
cards.helpful_note: Noise overload can trigger fight-or-flight. The plan should protect movement.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: This is unsafe now. We are leaving the sound before we talk.
cards.do_this: Move to a quieter space first. Repeat the same phrase if the child argues.
cards.helpful_note: Leaving the trigger is not failure. It is regulation.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: Safe body first. Quiet place next. Talk later.
cards.do_this: Use short instructions and reduce visual and sound input.
cards.helpful_note: Stacking demands can intensify overload.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: Let us review what noise level was too much and what helped.
cards.do_this: Identify the trigger and choose a tool or exit plan for next time.
cards.helpful_note: Reviewing while calm builds self-awareness.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: The noise overload was real. We still need to repair what happened during it.
cards.do_this: Repair harm or mess, then update the sensory plan.
cards.helpful_note: This avoids excusing unsafe behavior while supporting sensory needs.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: Next time, signal early so we can leave before your body panics.
cards.do_this: Choose a discreet signal and practice it once.
cards.helpful_note: Early signals preserve dignity and safety.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: The noise is overloading you. Use the exit or headphone plan now.
cards.do_this: Offer privacy and avoid making the teen explain in front of others.
cards.helpful_note: Dignity makes support easier to accept.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: You do not have to stay in a sound level your body cannot handle. Choose the reset.
cards.do_this: Give the teen control over the regulation tool within safe limits.
cards.helpful_note: Autonomy helps teens regulate without feeling embarrassed.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: Let us lower the input first. We can solve the rest after your body settles.
cards.do_this: Move to quiet, lower volume, or step outside before discussing behavior.
cards.helpful_note: Regulation before problem-solving keeps the conversation productive.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: I will help you get space. I cannot let anyone get hurt while we do it.
cards.do_this: Move away from crowds, traffic, and breakable items. Call for support if the situation keeps escalating.
cards.helpful_note: Noise overload can escalate quickly, so safety and space come first.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: This is not safe to discuss here. We are moving to a quieter place now.
cards.do_this: Keep the instruction brief and avoid public debate.
cards.helpful_note: Public arguments add shame and sensory load.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: Safety first. Quiet second. Conversation later.
cards.do_this: Use the shortest possible direction and reduce attention from others if possible.
cards.helpful_note: This protects dignity and reduces escalation.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: Let us make a plan so noise overload does not turn into a blowup next time.
cards.do_this: Agree on warning signs, tools, and a discreet exit plan.
cards.helpful_note: Teens need a plan they can use without feeling singled out.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: The sensory overload was real. The impact still matters.
cards.do_this: Name what needs repair and what support would have helped earlier.
cards.helpful_note: This combines accountability with accommodations.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: Next time, use the signal before it becomes unsafe.
cards.do_this: Choose a signal and decide what the parent will do when they see it.
cards.helpful_note: A shared signal lowers public conflict.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: Bath feels hard. We will wash one part, then pause.
cards.do_this: Lower the demand: wash hands, feet, or body with a warm cloth before trying more.
cards.helpful_note: Smaller steps help the body tolerate water and touch.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: Water feels too much. I will help you go slow.
cards.do_this: Let the child touch the water first, choose a cloth, or sit near the tub before getting in.
cards.helpful_note: Control over the first step can reduce panic.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: Your body needs gentle washing. We will keep it quick.
cards.do_this: Use calm movements, warm water, and very few words.
cards.helpful_note: Predictability makes body care feel safer.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: Stop. Wet bodies need safe feet. I will help you get steady.
cards.do_this: Prevent slipping, remove hard objects, and pause the bath if the child is thrashing.
cards.helpful_note: Water adds risk, so safety comes before finishing the wash.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: I will not let anyone get hurt in the bathroom. We are pausing.
cards.do_this: Drain water if needed, wrap in a towel, and move to a safer position.
cards.helpful_note: A wet, escalated child needs a safety reset.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: Bath can wait. Safe body first.
cards.do_this: Stop the routine and switch to the minimum safe cleanup if needed.
cards.helpful_note: The parent can protect hygiene without forcing a full bath during danger.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: Bath was hard for your body. Next time we will start smaller.
cards.do_this: Choose one bath support: cloth wash, less water, no hair wash, or a short timer.
cards.helpful_note: A gentler plan builds trust.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: You were scared or uncomfortable. We will make washing feel safer.
cards.do_this: Practice one calm step outside bath time, such as touching the cloth or choosing soap.
cards.helpful_note: Practice outside the hard moment lowers fear.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: We still care for your body. We will do it in a safer way.
cards.do_this: Name the next bath plan in one sentence and reconnect.
cards.helpful_note: Repair should not shame body-care struggles.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: Bath feels hard. Choose: washcloth first or water first.
cards.do_this: Offer one small choice and one clear expectation. Keep the routine short.
cards.helpful_note: A clear choice can lower resistance while keeping hygiene on track.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: Your body does not want the bath. We still need to get clean. Let us make it easier.
cards.do_this: Use a timer, favorite towel, lower water level, or split hair washing from body washing.
cards.helpful_note: Adjustments can reduce sensory distress without dropping the routine.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: We are not fighting the whole bath. We are doing the first small step.
cards.do_this: Start with one required step and pause. Avoid threatening consequences in the bathroom.
cards.helpful_note: Bathrooms already carry sensory intensity. Fewer words help.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: Wet feet need safety. I will not let running, throwing, or slipping happen.
cards.do_this: Move items out of reach, keep the floor dry, and pause the bath if unsafe.
cards.helpful_note: Bathroom safety has to come first.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: The bath is paused. Bodies have to be safe before we continue.
cards.do_this: Wrap the child in a towel and wait for the body to settle before deciding the minimum cleanup.
cards.helpful_note: A pause prevents injury and reduces panic.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: I hear that you do not want this. I will not let the bathroom become unsafe.
cards.do_this: Use a calm limit and stop negotiating while water or hard surfaces make risk higher.
cards.helpful_note: The environment changes the safety level.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: Bath got too big. Let us choose a better washing plan for next time.
cards.do_this: Pick one change: water level, washcloth, shower instead, bath instead, timer, or no hair wash that day.
cards.helpful_note: Planning gives the child safe control.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: The feeling was real. The unsafe part still needs repair.
cards.do_this: Help clean water, towels, or thrown items, then reconnect.
cards.helpful_note: Repair can happen without shaming body discomfort.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: Next time, say 'I need a smaller bath' before your body gets unsafe.
cards.do_this: Practice the phrase and define what a smaller bath means.
cards.helpful_note: Specific language makes the next request easier to honor.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: I hear that bathing feels hard. Hygiene is still happening. What is the most doable version?
cards.do_this: Offer acceptable versions: quick shower, washcloth, hair separate, timer, or privacy with check-in.
cards.helpful_note: Older kids need dignity and concrete choices around body care.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: Let us solve the hardest part: water, hair, soap, temperature, or privacy.
cards.do_this: Ask for the main barrier and adjust one thing while keeping the hygiene expectation.
cards.helpful_note: Specific problem-solving prevents a global refusal.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: Your body needs more control over this. Choose the first step.
cards.do_this: Give a start time and two acceptable options. Step back if privacy is safe.
cards.helpful_note: Respect and structure work better than pressure.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: The bathroom is not safe for throwing, running, or fighting. We are pausing.
cards.do_this: Remove items, address wet floors, and stop the conversation until the body is safe.
cards.helpful_note: Hard surfaces and water make escalation more dangerous.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: Hygiene matters. Safety comes first. We will reset and come back to the plan.
cards.do_this: Move out of the bathroom if safe and return later with a minimum hygiene step.
cards.helpful_note: The boundary remains, but the immediate method can change.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: I will not force this while bodies are unsafe. We are stopping the fight now.
cards.do_this: Do not corner or physically force bathing. Use space and a later reset.
cards.helpful_note: Forced body care can escalate fear and power struggles.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: We need a hygiene plan that respects your body and actually gets done.
cards.do_this: Agree on frequency, method, privacy, timing, and minimum steps.
cards.helpful_note: A practical plan reduces repeated arguments.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: The sensory part was real. The unsafe part still needs repair.
cards.do_this: Repair the space if needed, then adjust the routine.
cards.helpful_note: This keeps accountability and support together.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: Next time, ask for the adjustment before refusing the whole routine.
cards.do_this: Write the approved options: quick rinse, washcloth, hair later, timer, or water temperature choice.
cards.helpful_note: Pre-approved options reduce daily negotiations.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: I respect that body care can feel complicated. Hygiene still needs a workable plan.
cards.do_this: Offer privacy and ask the teen to choose the method and timing within agreed limits.
cards.helpful_note: Teen hygiene conversations go better when dignity comes first.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: This is not about control. It is about caring for your body. What is the plan you can follow?
cards.do_this: Ask for a specific time and minimum routine. Step out of the details if safety is not an issue.
cards.helpful_note: Ownership makes follow-through more likely.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: Let us make this doable without turning it into a fight.
cards.do_this: Collaborate on shower length, products, timing, and privacy needs.
cards.helpful_note: Respectful collaboration lowers defensiveness.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: I will not argue about hygiene while things are unsafe. We are pausing.
cards.do_this: End the confrontation, give space, and revisit the hygiene expectation later.
cards.helpful_note: Safety and dignity matter more than winning the moment.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: The plan can change. Unsafe behavior cannot continue.
cards.do_this: Do not follow, corner, or shame. Return with a simple expectation when calm.
cards.helpful_note: Escalation should not become the center of body care.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: We are stopping this conversation now. Safety first, hygiene plan later.
cards.do_this: Create space and reduce the audience. Document concerns if the pattern is persistent.
cards.helpful_note: A calm reset helps keep body care from becoming traumatic.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: We need a hygiene plan that protects your privacy and still gets care done.
cards.do_this: Agree on timing, minimum routine, supplies, and how reminders will happen.
cards.helpful_note: Clear plans reduce shame and repeated conflict.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: The refusal showed something is not working. The escalation still needs repair.
cards.do_this: Name what crossed the line and what support would help next time.
cards.helpful_note: This keeps the teen accountable without humiliation.

cards.category_subtitle: Meet the body where it is
cards.say_this: Next time, ask for an adjustment instead of letting it become a blowup.
cards.do_this: Make a short list of acceptable adjustments and consequences if the plan is ignored.
cards.helpful_note: Predictability reduces power struggles.

cards.helpful_note: Shame can make body accidents feel scary. Calm cleanup teaches safety.
cards.safety_note: If bedwetting is new, painful, frequent after a long dry period, or paired with other concerning symptoms, consider contacting the child's medical provider.
